description: This figure plots the strain hardening rate (unit: MPa) as a function of true strain for three low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels, with x = 0.5 (black curve, 0.5C steel), 0.8 (red curve, 0.8C steel), and 1.2 (green curve, 1.2C steel) wt.% carbon. All three samples present an initial strain hardening rate close to 4000 MPa at very low strain. The 0.5C and 0.8C steels show continuously decreasing strain hardening rates with rising strain, dropping to 0 at ~0.27 and ~0.32 strain, respectively. In comparison, the 1.2C steel exhibits three-stage strain hardening: after an initial sharp decrease to ~1650 MPa at ~0.05 strain, its strain hardening rate recovers to a plateau of ~1900 MPa, and only declines sharply to 0 at ~0.45 strain. The results verify that higher carbon content extends the strain hardening capacity of the steels, corresponding to better ductility, and the distinct strain hardening behaviors are associated with their different microstructures and deformation mechanisms.
